name: CeO2 - O
synthesis_para: To obtain CeO2–O and CeO2-R, 3.47 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.03 g Na3PO4·12H2O (A.R, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), as well as 1.39 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 15.36 g NaOH(A.R, National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), were separately dissolved in 64 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 1 h and 30 min, respectively. Subsequent to this, both mixtures were transferred to an autoclave and subjected to the hydrothermal processes at 160 °C and 100 °C for 24 h, respectively. The obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - C
synthesis_para: The CeO2 nano-cube (CeO2–C) was prepared using the hydrothermal method according the procedure reported by Cao et al. [47]. The specific procedure involved dissolving 3.47 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O in 20 mL of ultrapure water, while 13.71 g NaOH was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the obtained NaOH solution was added dropwise to above cerium nitrate solution under vigorous stirring. After 30 min of vigorous stirring, the mixed solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - P
synthesis_para: The CeO2 powder (CeO2–P) was synthesized following the procedure reported by Zheng et al. [48]. In this method, 2.17 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 4 g urea (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.), and 9.8 g NaOH were combined in 40 mL of ultrapure water and vigorously stirred for 25 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to an oven at 120 °C for 24 h hydrothermal reaction. The obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - S
synthesis_para: For CeO2–S, 1.99 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (A.R, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) and 0.8 g of PVP (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in a specific amount of ethylene glycol (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.). Subsequently, 8 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Following this, the concoction underwent hydrothermal reaction in an autoclave at 160 °C for 24 h and the obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CuCe0.7Zr0.3
synthesis_para: A series of CuO-CexZr1-xOy catalysts were prepared by a novel surfactant assisted grinding method. In a typical procedure, appropriate amounts of nitrate salts of Cu, Ce and Zr, and oxalic acid (the molar ratio of oxalic acid to metal ions = 1.7:1; the content of CuO was fixed at 10 wt% based on our previous study [28]) were manually ground in an agate mortar for 5 min. Then, an appropriate amount of surfactant PEG 600 was added to the mixture and ground for further 25 min. After that, all the components became a uniform, viscous and muddy precursor. Then, it was dried at 65 ◦C for 24 h, calcined in air at 500 ◦C for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 ◦C/min to obtain the CuO-CexZr1-xOy catalysts. The molar contents of Ce in the CexZr1-xOy were x = 1, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3 and 0, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CuCe, CuCe0.7Zr0.3, CuCe0.5Zr0.5, CuCe0.3Zr0.7 and CuZr, respectively.

name: RT-CeO2
synthesis_para: The chemicals, cerium nitrate hexahydrate, Ce(NO3)3·6H2Oand ammonia were purchased from Aldrich (Germany, purity 99.99%). CeO2 nanoparticles were prepared by a process that involves the homogeneous precipitation from cerium salt solution with dilute aqueous ammonia. In the case of ceria synthesised at room temperature, the requisite quantity of precursor was dissolved in double-distilled water and was precipitated by dropwise addition of the solution to aqueous ammonia over a period. Excess precipitant was maintained during the process. The obtained light yellow coloured slurry was washed 10 times with deionized water to remove any water-soluble species and subsequently washed 3 times with ethanol to remove water and disrupt hydrogen bonded networks between water and the precursor, which otherwise led to severe aggregation upon heating. The collected precipitate was oven-dried at 110 ​°C for 12 ​h, crushed using an agate mortar, and subsequently calcined at 500 ​°C for 3 ​h in an air atmosphere to obtain the oxide material (RT-Ce)

name: HT-CeO2
synthesis_para: The chemicals, cerium nitrate hexahydrate, Ce(NO3)3·6H2Oand ammonia were purchased from Aldrich (Germany, purity 99.99%). CeO2 nanoparticles were prepared by a process that involves the homogeneous precipitation from cerium salt solution with dilute aqueous ammonia. In the case of ceria synthesised at room temperature, the requisite quantity of precursor was dissolved in double-distilled water and was precipitated by dropwise addition of the solution to aqueous ammonia over a period. Excess precipitant was maintained during the process. The obtained light yellow coloured slurry was washed 10 times with deionized water to remove any water-soluble species and subsequently washed 3 times with ethanol to remove water and disrupt hydrogen bonded networks between water and the precursor, which otherwise led to severe aggregation upon heating. The collected precipitate obtained after ammonia addition to the precursor solution was immediately transferred into a tightly sealed stainless steel vessel and kept in an air oven maintained at 130 ​°C for 24 ​h. The solid collected was dried at 110 ​°C in the air oven and calcined at 500 ​°C for 3 ​h to yield the oxide (HT-Ce).

name: CeO2
synthesis_para: The different mixed oxide catalysts were prepared by slightly modified two-steps solution combustion synthesis using Ce(NO3)3.6H2O and Cu(NO3)2.3H2O as oxidizers and citric acid (C6H8O7) as a fuel [23]. For the synthesis of CeO2, CuO and CexCux−1O2 mixed oxides, appropriate volumes of 0.5 M stock solutions of cerium nitrate, copper nitrate and citric acid, all dissolved in ultrapure deionized water, were mixed together. For the synthesis of CexCu1−xO2−δ, a suitable amount of copper nitrate was used to obtain catalysts with the general formula CexCu1−xO2−δ, where x = 0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1. For all catalysts, the amount of citric acid was chosen so that the mole ratio of the citric acid to the total metal ions is 1.5:1 (slight excess of citric acid). The reaction mixture of the metal nitrate precursors and the fuel were heated under stirring in a beaker placed in a sand-bath over a hotplate at 90 °C. The reaction mixture was left under heating and stirring till evaporation of excess water and formation of gel-like material. The gel-like material formed was then transferred to a muffle furnace pre-heated to 380 °C and was kept at this temperature for 4 h leading to combustion and formation of spongy solid product. The spongy solid product was calcined in static air at 550 °C for 4 h with heating rate of 5 °C/min. Finally, the resulting solid was ground to fine powder and used as is for characterization and catalytic testing.

name: CPS
synthesis_para: The optimized Ce0.80Hf0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CHS), Ce0.80Zr0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CZS), Ce0.80Pr0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CPS), and Ce0.80La0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CLS) (mol ratio based on oxides) ternary oxide solid solutions were synthesized by a coprecipitation method. In detail, the desired quantities (see Table S1, ESI†) of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, SnCl4·5H2O, and other respective metal precursors such as HfCl4, ZrO(NO3)2·xH2O, Pr(NO3)3·6H2O, and La(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in distilled water separately and then mixed together for 1 h. Under constant stirring, aqueous ammonia solution was added dropwise to the mixture until the precipitation was complete, at which point the pH was approximately 9.0. The resulting suspensions were stirred for another 12 h, aged for 24 h and then vacuum filtered. Afterwards, the precipitates were washed thoroughly with distilled water and the obtained solids were dried at 373 K overnight and finally calcined at 773 K for 5 h. For comparison, the same synthesis route was employed for the preparation of CeO2.

name: Mn1Ce1
synthesis_para: MnOx-CeO2 catalysts with different Mn/Ce mole ratios (0.33–2) were prepared by a redox co-precipitation method [34]. The KMnO4 precursor dissolved in deionized water, in a 10% stoichiometric excess, was titrated with a solution of Ce(NO3)3 and Mn(NO3)2 at 60 °C under vigorous stirring, keeping pH = 8.0 ± 0.3 using a 0.2 M KOH solution. After titration, the solid was digested for 30 min at 60 °C and then filtered, repeatedly washed with hot distilled water, and dried overnight at 100 °C. Aliquots of the dried samples were further calcined in air at 400 °C (6 h).  N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were collected on an Autosorb-6 at −198 °C.

name: Meso-CeO2
synthesis_para: The various meso-MO x materials such as CeO2, Mn2O3, Co3O4, SnO2 and TiO2 were synthesized by the nano-replication method. Typically, the KIT-6 template was heated at 373K for 1h. Then, each precursor (Ce(NO3)·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, CO(NO3)2·6H2O, SnCl2·6H2O, and Ti(OCH(CH3)2)4) was dissolved in distilled water, and impregnated into the silica template (see Table S1 about the detailed amounts of each precursor solution). Subsequently, the composites were put in an oven at 80°C overnight for the spontaneous infiltration of the precursor into the mesopores of the silica template. The each composite was heated at 450°C, 550°C, 550°C, 700°C and 450°C, respectively, under static air conditions for 3h. After the heat treatment, the silica template was removed using 2M NaOH solution 2 times. Finally, the meso-MO x materials thus obtained were washed with distilled water and acetone several times and dried at room temperature.

name: CeO2
synthesis_para: Nanosized copper cerium bimetal oxides were prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 1.6mmol of copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) and 6.4mmol of cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) were dissolved in 20mL of deionized water. In all cases, the concentrations of the total metal ions (i.e., [Cu(II)]+[Ce(III)]) were kept constant at 0.4M. 14.4mmol of NaOH solution (0.15M) was added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 10min, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and rinsed with distilled water for removing the excess metal ions by vacuum filtration. Then it was dispersed into 80mL deionized water and transferred into a 100mL PTFE autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was placed in an oven and maintained at 120°C for 5h. After the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the precipitate was dried at 80°C for 12h and then calcined at 500°C for 3h in air. The product was named as Ce0.80Cu0.20O2. For comparison, the percent of Cu doping content was varied from 0 to 25mol% and the calcination temperature was also varied from 300 to 600°C.

name: CeO2-CuO
synthesis_para: The CeO2-MOx (M=Cu, Mn, Fe, Co, and Ni) materials were prepared by a citric acid complexation-combustion method[14]. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·2H2O (or Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·6H2O and Ni(NO3)2·9H2O) were dissolved in de-ionized water with M/Ce=1/9 (molar ratio). Citric acid (Citric acid/M=1.2) was added in the nitrate salts aqueous solution. The synthesis solution was stirred at 70 °C till this solution was changed into gel. Once the gel formed, this gel was quickly elevated to 150 °C, resulting in the gel foam and sparks formation. After the sparks were extinguished, a solid product was obtained. Then the obtained powder was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h in air and ground into 40–60 mesh.

Once the gel formed, this gel was quickly elevated to 150 °C, resulting in the gel foam and sparks formation. After the sparks were extinguished, a solid product was obtained. Then the obtained powder was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h in air and ground into 40–60 mesh.

name: Cu0.09C0.91eOx
synthesis_para: In a typical synthetic procedure, 2–6 mmol of ammonium cerium(IV) nitrate and an appropriate amount of the acetylacetonate salts of the dopant metal (Cu, Sm, and Co) were dissolved in 20 mL of oleyl amine. The reaction mixture was heated up to 100 °C under gentle magnetic stirring until the formation of a clear solution. Subsequently, the reaction temperature was raised to 300 °C and remained at this temperature for 1 h. Thereafter, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and the nanoparticles were precipitated by the addition of a large excess of ethanol. The precipitate was isolated by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min. To remove the excess unbonded oleyl amine molecules and other reaction byproducts, the particles were redissolved in chloroform, precipitated by ethanol, and isolated by centrifugation. The above procedure was repeated three times. Finally, the surface-bonded oleyl amine molecules were removed by a calcination step at 450 °C for 2 h under a continuous air flow. The methodology is characterized by simplicity, repeatability, and the ability to synthesize monodispersed, up to a 1 g scale, nanoparticle quantities.

name: NiCeOx
synthesis_para: The microwave-accelerated reaction system (MARS-6) for material synthesis was used in this study and has been described elsewhere. (18) The doped-ceria materials were prepared by dissolving the precursor salts Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and M(NO3)3·xH2O (M = metal heteroatom) in distilled water with the respective molar ratios for obtaining the final composition of M–Ce–O, where M: Fe, Mn, Ni, Co, Zn, Cu, and Zr and nominal M/Ce ratio: 0.25. Citric acid was used as a complexing agent. Calcination in static air, ca. 500 °C for 6 h under atmospheric pressure, was performed in all samples. More details on the applied synthesis and calcination procedures applied are provided in the Supporting Information.

name: CeZrCu10
synthesis_para: The CuO/Ce0.8Zr0.2O2 catalysts with different CuO content were synthesized using a surfactant-assisted method of nanoparticle assembly. At room temperature, 6 mmol of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved into 200 ml distilled water under ultrasound irradiation for 15 min, then 8 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 2 mmol of Zr(NO3)4·5H2O and calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were added under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 0.5 h, 0.2 mol/l sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added into the above solution until the pH value of the mixed solution was 10, and then further stirred for about 12 h. The final suspended solution was aged at 90 °C for 3 h, washed with hot water, dried in the oven at 110 °C for 6 h, then milled and calcined at 400 °C for 4 h. The content of CuO was 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CeZrCu0, CeZrCu5, CeZrCu10, CeZrCu15, CeZrCu20, CeZrCu25, CeZrCu30, CeZrCu40, respectively. In order to make clear the influence of the calcination temperature on the catalyst property, a series of CeZrCu25 catalysts calcined at different temperature were prepared in the similar manner.
description: mesoporous nanocrystalline particle particles nanoscaled mesoporous nanostructured

name: CeSnO–0.9
synthesis_para: A set of CeXSn1−XO2−δ mixed oxides (X ranging from 0.975 to 0.80) were prepared by co-precipitation method, from cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and tin chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) as precursors. The resultant solids were repeatedly washed, dried and calcined at 500 °C for 5 h in air. The synthesis of the mixed oxides was also carried out at pH = 9 and room temperature, baking the resultant suspension for 1 h. The resultant slurry was filtered and washed several times with deionized water, dried overnight at 115 °C and calcined in air at 500 °C for 5 h. The obtained solids were denoted as CeSnO–X, being X the mol fraction of ceria in the mixed oxide.

name: Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ
synthesis_para: The nanocrystalline solid solution catalyst, Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ, was prepared using a single-step combustion method [12]. All the metal precursors and chemicals used in the preparation of the catalysts were procured from Sigma-Aldrich with purity 99.9%. In the preparation, a redox combustion mixture was composed of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, Co(NO3)2.5H2O and NH2CONH2 in the ratio of 0.90: 0.1: 3.77 respectively. Typically, a mixture of 9.869 g of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, 0.582 g of Co(NO3)2.5H2O and 4.529 g of NH2CONH2 was dissolved in 50 cm3 of water in a borosilicate dish. The resulting solution was heated at 150 °C on a hotplate for 10 min. The boiling solution was then transferred into a muffle furnace, preheated to 400 °C. After almost complete dehydration, the slurry ignited, resulting in the formation of a brown solid product. A similar one-step combustion method was used to prepare the Ce0.90-xCuxCo0.10O2–δ (where x = 0.01, 0.03 and 0.05) bimetallic solid solutions, using Cu(NO3)2.3H2O as a precursor, in addition to the other starting materials mentioned in the preparation of Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ.

name: Ce0.85Cu0.05Co0.10O2-δ
synthesis_para: The nanocrystalline solid solution catalyst, Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ, was prepared using a single-step combustion method [12]. All the metal precursors and chemicals used in the preparation of the catalysts were procured from Sigma-Aldrich with purity 99.9%. In the preparation, a redox combustion mixture was composed of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, Co(NO3)2.5H2O and NH2CONH2 in the ratio of 0.90: 0.1: 3.77 respectively. Typically, a mixture of 9.869 g of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, 0.582 g of Co(NO3)2.5H2O and 4.529 g of NH2CONH2 was dissolved in 50 cm3 of water in a borosilicate dish. The resulting solution was heated at 150 °C on a hotplate for 10 min. The boiling solution was then transferred into a muffle furnace, preheated to 400 °C. After almost complete dehydration, the slurry ignited, resulting in the formation of a brown solid product. A similar one-step combustion method was used to prepare the Ce0.90-xCuxCo0.10O2–δ (where x = 0.01, 0.03 and 0.05) bimetallic solid solutions, using Cu(NO3)2.3H2O as a precursor, in addition to the other starting materials mentioned in the preparation of Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ.

name: 10-CC
synthesis_para: A series of CuO–CeO2 catalysts with different CuO contents were synthesized via a direct solvothermal method. Typically, specified amounts of Cu and Ce nitrates were dissolved in EG. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave of 80 mL capacity and heated at 180 °C for 3 h with continuous stirring. Then the obtained suspension was naturally cooled down to room temperature (RT). The products thus obtained were separated by filtration, then washed with deionized water and ethanol, dried at 120 °C overnight and calcined under static air at 450 °C (based on our previous study28) for 4 h. The CuO loadings of these catalysts were 0, 5, 10, 20, and 30 mol% expressed as the mole ratio of Cu/(Cu + Ce), which were denoted as CeO2, 5-CC, 10-CC, 20-CC, and 30-CC, respectively.

name: Ce0.75Zr0.25
synthesis_para: CeO2–ZrO2 mixed oxide catalysts were prepared by using the sol–gel technique. Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (99.0%), Fluka), and zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O (99.0%), Fluka) were used as sources of Ce and Zr, respectively. The starting solution was prepared by mixing 0.1 M of metal salt solutions with 0.4 M of urea solution at a 2 to 1 volumetric ratio. The ratio between each metal salt was altered depending on the desired solid solution concentration: Ce1−xZrxO2,where x=0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1.0. The solution was kept at 100°C for either 50 or 120 h. The sample was then allowed to cool to room temperature before being centrifuged to separate the solid product from the solution. The solid product was washed with ethanol to prevent an agglomeration of the particles, and then dried overnight in an oven at 110°C followed by calcination at either 500 or 900°C for 4 h in air.

name: CeO2−La2O3 773
synthesis_para: The CeO2−La2O3 (8:2 mol ratio based on oxides) solid solution was synthesized by a modified coprecipitation method from ultrahigh dilute solutions. This method is more attracting because of its relative simplicity, environmental friendliness, economical effectiveness, and suitability for large scale production. (29) Coprecipitation was carried out from Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) and La(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) precursors, and a dilute aqueous ammonia solution was used as the precipitating agent. The requisite quantities of precursors were dissolved separately in double distilled water and mixed together under continuous stirring. The mixed solution was precipitated by dropwise addition of the aqueous ammonia solution over a period until the solution pH reached ∼8.5. The obtained light yellow colored slurry was decanted, filtered, and washed several times with doubly distilled water until free from anion impurities. The collected precipitate was oven-dried at 393 K for 12 h, crushed using an agate mortar, and subsequently calcined at 773 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1 in air atmosphere to obtain the mixed oxide material. Finally, some portions of the finished catalyst were further heated at 873, 973, and 1073 K for 5 h using the same heating ramp.

name: Ce0.8Cu0.2O2
synthesis_para: Nanosized copper cerium bimetal oxides were prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 1.6mmol of copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) and 6.4mmol of cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) were dissolved in 20mL of deionized water. In all cases, the concentrations of the total metal ions (i.e., [Cu(II)]+[Ce(III)]) were kept constant at 0.4M. 14.4mmol of NaOH solution (0.15M) was added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 10min, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and rinsed with distilled water for removing the excess metal ions by vacuum filtration. Then it was dispersed into 80mL deionized water and transferred into a 100mL PTFE autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was placed in an oven and maintained at 120°C for 5h. After the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the precipitate was dried at 80°C for 12h and then calcined at 500°C for 3h in air. The product was named as Ce0.80Cu0.20O2. For comparison, the percent of Cu doping content was varied from 0 to 25mol% and the calcination temperature was also varied from 300 to 600°C.

name: CeSnO–1
synthesis_para: Both pure CeO2 and SnO2 were prepared by precipitation with ammonium hydroxide from Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and SnCl4·5H2O precursors, respectively. Pure oxides were synthesized at room temperature and pH = 9, with dropwise addition of ammonium hydroxide, under magnetic stirring for 1 h. To dissolve SnCl4·5H2O precursor, hydrochloric acid was added into magnetically stirred solution until pH = 1.0, at which value the solution became transparent. The resultant slurry was filtered and washed several times with deionized water, dried overnight at 115 °C and calcined in air at 500 °C for 5 h.

name: CeSnO–0.975
synthesis_para: A set of CeXSn1−XO2−δ mixed oxides (X ranging from 0.975 to 0.80) were prepared by co-precipitation method, from cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and tin chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) as precursors. The resultant solids were repeatedly washed, dried and calcined at 500 °C for 5 h in air. The synthesis of the mixed oxides was also carried out at pH = 9 and room temperature, baking the resultant suspension for 1 h. The resultant slurry was filtered and washed several times with deionized water, dried overnight at 115 °C and calcined in air at 500 °C for 5 h. The obtained solids were denoted as CeSnO–X, being X the mol fraction of ceria in the mixed oxide.

name: CeSnO–0.80
synthesis_para: A set of CeXSn1−XO2−δ mixed oxides (X ranging from 0.975 to 0.80) were prepared by co-precipitation method, from cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and tin chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) as precursors. name: CeO2
synthesis_para: MnOx-CeO2 catalysts with different Mn/Ce mole ratios (0.33–2) were prepared by a redox co-precipitation method [34]. The KMnO4 precursor dissolved in deionized water, in a 10% stoichiometric excess, was titrated with a solution of Ce(NO3)3 and Mn(NO3)2 at 60 °C under vigorous stirring, keeping pH = 8.0 ± 0.3 using a 0.2 M KOH solution. After titration, the solid was digested for 30 min at 60 °C and then filtered, repeatedly washed with hot distilled water, and dried overnight at 100 °C. Aliquots of the dried samples were further calcined in air at 400 °C (6 h).  N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were collected on an Autosorb-6 at −198 °C.

name: Ce95Mn5
synthesis_para: Pure cerium, manganese and copper oxides, as well as binary (Ce–Mn, Ce–Cu) and ternary oxide catalysts (Ce–Mn–Cu) were prepared through the solution combustion synthesis (SCS) [13, 19, 23,24,25]. The mixed oxide catalysts were prepared with different foreign metal contents: 5, 15 and 45 at.%. The notation is: Ce100−xMx, where M represents either Mn or Cu for the binary oxides, and Ce100−xMnx/2Cux/2 for the ternary oxides. Therefore, the list of powder catalysts prepared in the current work is: CeO2, Ce95Mn5, Ce85Mn15, Ce55Mn45, Mn2O3, Ce95Cu5, Ce85Cu15, Ce55Cu45, CuO, Ce95Mn2.5Cu2.5, Ce85Mn7.5Cu7.5, Ce55Mn22.5Cu22.5. During a typical synthesis, proper amounts of metal precursors, namely Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar); Mn(NO3)2·4H2O; Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (Sigma-Aldrich), were dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water along with a “fuel” (urea), whose amount was made in excess (200% of the stoichiometric amount). The amounts of precursors were defined to have nominal metal-to-cerium ratios. The mixture was stirred vigorously at r.t. for 5 min. Then, the homogeneous aqueous solution was heated in oven at 650 °C for 30 min.

During a typical synthesis, proper amounts of metal precursors, namely Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar); Mn(NO3)2·4H2O; Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (Sigma-Aldrich), were dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water along with a “fuel” (urea), whose amount was made in excess (200% of the stoichiometric amount). The amounts of precursors were defined to have nominal metal-to-cerium ratios. The mixture was stirred vigorously at r.t. for 5 min. Then, the homogeneous aqueous solution was heated in oven at 650 °C for 30 min.

name: CeO2
synthesis_para: Two microemulsion systems with the composition given in Table 1 were prepared separately. The first one, ME1, contains the adequate amounts of the metal nitrates in the aqueous solution, and the second one, ME2, contains the adequate amount of the precipitating agent (NH3) in the aqueous solution. Then, ME1 was added dropwise to the ME2 and precipitated particles were obtained. The mixture was kept under vigorous stirring during 24 h at room temperature and after that, the particles were collected by centrifugation. The surfactant, cosurfactant and oil phase excesses were washed first with a 50/50 methanol/chloroform mixture and then with methanol. The obtained solid was dried at 60 °C during 24 h and finally calcined (10 °C/min) at 500 °C for 2 h.

name: Mn-CeO2
synthesis_para: Mn–CeO2, Co–CeO2, and Ni–CeO2 NRs were synthesized using manganese(ii) nitrate tetrahydrate [Mn(NO3)2·4H2O], nickel(ii) nitrate hexahydrate [Ni(NO3)2·6H2O], cobalt(ii) nitrate hexahydrate [Co(NO3)2·6H2O], and cerium(iii) nitrate hexahydrate [Ce(NO3)3·6H2O] precursors. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, AR grade. For Mn–CeO2 NRs, 1.88 mmol (0.816 g) of cerium(iii) nitrate hexahydrate was mixed with Milli-Q water (20 mL) by stirring until a homogeneous solution was obtained. Then, the required amount of Mn(NO3)2·4H2O was mixed to the Ce(NO3)3·6H2O solution. The Ce/Mn molar ratio was maintained at 9 : 1. NaOH (50 mL) was used as a precipitating agent and it was added slowly to the above solution and kept stirring 1 hour. The resultant precipitate was transformed to Teflon autoclave (100 mL) and it was kept at 100 °C for 24 h. After reaction, the collected precipitate was washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol before drying in air at 70 °C for 4 h. The dried powder was calcined in air at 400 °C for 3 h to obtain 10% Mn doped CeO2 NRs. Similarly, Co–CeO2 NRs, Ni–CeO2 NRs, and pure CeO2 NRs were prepared. The as-prepared Ce0.9Mn0.1O2−δ, Ce0.9Co0.1O2−δ, and Ce0.9Ni0.1O2−δ catalysts are represented as Mn–CeO2, Co–CeO2, and Ni–CeO2, respectively.

name: La-CeO2
synthesis_para: The M (M = La, Pr, Nd, and Pm)-doped CeO2 catalysts were synthesized by the citric-aided sol–gel method. In this method, lanthanum nitrate [La(NO3)3·6H2O], praseodymium nitrate [Pr(NO3)3·6H2O], neodymium nitrate [Nd(NO3)3·6H2O], promethium nitrate [Pm(NO3)3·6H2O], and cerium nitrate [Ce(NO3)4·6H2O] were used as La, Pr, Nd, and Ce precursors [(mol ratio of M(NO3)3·6H2O:Ce(NO3)4·6H2O = 20:80)]. Citric acid was used as a gelating agent. The required amount of metal nitrates and citric acid was dissolved in propan-2-ol (mol ratio of metal nitrates to citric acid is 1.5). After mixing, the above solution was heated at around 80 °C on a magnetic stirrer until it produced spongy yellow gel. The spongy gel was cooled at room temperature and then dried in an oven at 110 °C for 15 h. The obtained product was crushed properly and calcined at 300 °C for 4 h. The calcined samples were designated as La-CeO2, Pr-CeO2, Nd-CeO2, and Pm-CeO2.

name: Ce0.95Mn0.05
synthesis_para: Four ceria-based samples were prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis: CeO2, Ce0.95Mn0.05 (Mn/Ce atomic ratio = 1/19), Ce0.95Cu0.05 (Cu/Ce atomic ratio = 1/19) and Ce0.95Mn0.025Cu0.025 (Mn/Cu/Ce atomic ratio = 1/1/38). Subscripts indicate the atomic fractions of the metal cations. Briefly, stoichiometric quantities of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) and Cu(NO3)2·5H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) and/or Mn(NO3)2·4H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved in 10 ml of deionised water (solution A). Then, 48 g of NaOH were dissolved in 70 ml of deionized water (solution B). The salt precursor solution (A) was then added drop by drop into the hydroxide solution (B). The final mixture (A + B) was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. The final slurry was then poured into a 150-ml Teflon liner, that was later secured in an autoclave. The autoclave was heated in an oven for an aging treatment at 180 °C for 24 h. The resulted precipitate was washed with ethanol and deionised water and then dried overnight at 60 °C. Finally, the dry powder was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h.

name: PDC
synthesis_para: Ce0.8RE0.2O2−δ (RE = Ce, Pr, Sm, and Nd) NPs with a diameter of 12.8 ± 0.1 nm were synthesized by an EDTA–citrate complexing method. (46)Figure S8 describes the overall synthetic process in this study. Stoichiometric amounts of nitrate-based, cation precursors were dissolved in deionized water, and a mixture of EDTA and citric acid, serving as a chelating agent and fuel for combustion, was added. Subsequently, the pH values of the mixed solution were adjusted in the range of 4–10, using NH3·H2O. The EDTA/citric acid/total cation ion molar ratios were fixed at 1:2:1. The solution was then evaporated on a hot-plate at 80 °C and then heated further to 260 °C to form a crispy solid precursor. The combustion of the solid precursor was conducted in a heating mantle at 450 °C for 1 h, followed by calcination in a box furnace at 750 °C for 3 h.

name: CM-773
synthesis_para: An optimized nanocrystalline ceria–manganese (CM, 70:30 mol% based on oxides; Ce1−xMnxO2−δ, x = 0.3) mixed oxide was prepared by a coprecipitation method from ultrahigh dilute aqueous solutions using cerium(III) nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Aldrich, AR grade) and manganese(II) nitrate (Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Merck, AR grade) hydrate precursors. The desired amounts of precursors were dissolved separately in double distilled water under mild stirring conditions and mixed together. While stirring this mixture, aqueous ammonia solution was added dropwise into the mixed solution (pH = ∼9.5) so that a brownish colour precipitate was formed, and the stirring was continued constantly for 24 h. Then, the obtained precipitate was filtered off and repeatedly washed with double distilled water until free from anion impurities. The resultant precipitate was oven dried overnight at 373 K and then the dried solid was ground with ceramic mortar. Finally, the well-dispersed powder was calcined for 5 h (the heating and cooling rates were in the steps of 5 K min−1) in a closed electric furnace at 773 K to decompose the residual nitrates to favour the formation of the fluorite phase. Some portions of the calcined powder were again heated for 5 h at 873, 973, and 1073 K, respectively. The synthesized catalysts were designated as CM-T, where T represents the calcination temperature in K. For comparison, pure CeO2 and MnOx were also prepared by same method and denoted as C-773 and Mn-773, respectively..

name: Ce0.95Cu0.05O2-δ
synthesis_para: Ce1-x(TM1TM2)xO2-δ (TM1 = Cu, TM2 = Cu, Ag, or Ru) samples weresynthesized by the EDTA-citrate complexing method.[65–66] First, Ce(NO3)3 · 6H2O (Kanto Chem) was dissolved in deionized water so asto prepare a nitrate solution. The cation precursors (CuN2O6 · 3H2O(Sigma-Aldrich), AgNO3 (Sigma-Aldrich), or Cl3Ru · xH2O (Sigma-Aldrich)) to be substituted into the lattice were then dissolved. Inorder to prepare the EDTA-NH3 · H2O buffer solution, EDTA (JunseiChem) was dissolved in ammonia solution (NH3 · H2O, Assay min.28.0 %). Next, nitrate solution and EDTA-NH3 · H2O buffer solutionwere mixed together, then citric acid was added to the solution;the molar ratio of total cations was 1 : 1 : 2 (cation: EDTA: citric acid).The pH value of the mixed solution was adjusted to pH 10 bytitration with the ammonia solution. The solution was evaporatedon a hot-plate at 80 °C, then heated to 260 °C to form a crispy solid.The crispy solid was burned out at 450 °C for 2 h, then finallycalcined in a box furnace at 500 °C for 3 h.

The solution was evaporatedon a hot-plate at 80 °C, then heated to 260 °C to form a crispy solid.The crispy solid was burned out at 450 °C for 2 h, then finallycalcined in a box furnace at 500 °C for 3 h.

name: Ce0.95Cu0.04Ru0.01O2-δ
synthesis_para: Ce1-x(TM1TM2)xO2-δ (TM1 = Cu, TM2 = Cu, Ag, or Ru) samples weresynthesized by the EDTA-citrate complexing method.[65–66] First, Ce(NO3)3 · 6H2O (Kanto Chem) was dissolved in deionized water so asto prepare a nitrate solution. name: Ce0.95Mn0.05
synthesis_para: Four ceria-based samples were prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis: CeO2, Ce0.95Mn0.05 (Mn/Ce atomic ratio = 1/19), Ce0.95Cu0.05 (Cu/Ce atomic ratio = 1/19) and Ce0.95Mn0.025Cu0.025 (Mn/Cu/Ce atomic ratio = 1/1/38). Subscripts indicate the atomic fractions of the metal cations. Briefly, stoichiometric quantities of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) and Cu(NO3)2·5H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) and/or Mn(NO3)2·4H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved in 10 ml of deionised water (solution A). Then, 48 g of NaOH were dissolved in 70 ml of deionized water (solution B). The salt precursor solution (A) was then added drop by drop into the hydroxide solution (B). The final mixture (A + B) was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. The final slurry was then poured into a 150-ml Teflon liner, that was later secured in an autoclave. The autoclave was heated in an oven for an aging treatment at 180 °C for 24 h. The resulted precipitate was washed with ethanol and deionised water and then dried overnight at 60 °C. Finally, the dry powder was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h.

name: np-Mn0.08Ce0.92
synthesis_para: An ethanol (40 mL) solution of adipic acid (1.75 g, 12 mmol) and N,N,N-triethylenediamine (TEA, 4.2 mL, 30 mmol) was added to an ethanol solution (40 mL) of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (3.47 g, 8 mmol) while being vigorously stirred. A white precipitate was formed soon after the two solutions were mixed. After the solution had been vigorously stirred for 2 h, the synthesized product was collected by centrifugation (5000 rpm for 10 min). The as-obtained sample was washed several times with fresh ethanol. After the sample had been dried in vacuo at 80 °C overnight, 2.39 g of Ce-aph-CP was obtained as a white powder. Yield (based on the amount of Ce3+ used): 76%. FT-IR (ATR, cm–1): νOH = 3345(br); νC–H(aliphatic) = 2937, 2870(w); νO–C═O(carboxylate) = 1522, 1406(s). Anal. Calcd for Ce2C18H32O16 [Ce2(adipate)3·4H2O]: C, 27.55; H, 4.11; N, 0.00. Found: C, 27.24; H, 3.76; N, 0.00. The synthetic procedures for the other bimetallic CPs were the same, except for the amounts of precursors. A stoichiometric amount of adipic acid and 2.5 times the stoichiometric amount of TEA were used. The amounts of metal precursors and ligands are summarized in Table S1. For Mn0.11Ce0.89-aph-CP, 2.35 g of white powder was obtained. The as-synthesized bimetallic CP (1.00 g) was ground into a powder and then heated at a rate of 5 °C/min under a N2 (g) flow rate of 100 mL/min. The material was maintained at the target temperature of 500 °C for 6 h. After the sample had cooled to room temperature, a second heat treatment was used to remove the organic species to activate the preformed pores. The material was heated in an O2 (g) flow of 100 mL/min at a rate of 1 °C/min and maintained at the target temperature of 300 °C for 3 h. After the sample had cooled to room temperature, the desired solid was obtained. All solids obtained after N2 treatment were black, whereas the resultant np-CeO2, np-Mn0.08Ce0.92O2−δ, np-Mn0.18Ce0.82O2−δ, np-Mn0.13Ce0.87O2−δ·MnOy, np-Ni0.08Ce0.92O2−δ, np-Co0.08Ce0.92O2−δ, and np-Fe0.09Ce0.91O2−δ after O2 treatments were pale yellow, brown, dark brown, dark brown, dark brown, light brown, and brown powders, respectively.

name: CZ-773
synthesis_para: The investigated CeO2–ZrO2 (CZ, 1:1), CeO2–La2O3 (CL, 8:2), CeO2–Eu2O3 (CE, 8:2), and CeO2–Sm2O3 (CS, 8:2) in different mole ratios of oxide solid solutions and a reference CeO2 were synthesized by a modified coprecipitation method using appropriate amounts of the corresponding Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade), ZrO(NO3)2·xH2O (Fluka, AR grade), La(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade), Eu(NO3)3·5H2O (Aldrich, AR grade), and Sm(NO3)3·6H2O precursors respectively. The desired amounts of the precursors were dissolved separately in double distilled water under mild stirring conditions and mixed together. Dilute aqueous ammonia solution was added drop wise over a period until the pH of the solution reached ∼8.5. The resulting pale yellow coloured slurry was decanted, filtered, and washed several times with double distilled water until free from anion impurities. The obtained precipitates were oven dried at 393 K for 12 h and calcined at 773, 1073, and 1273 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1 in air atmosphere. In future, for convenience CeO2–ZrO2, CeO2–La2O3, CeO2–Eu2O3, CeO2–Sm2O3, and CeO2 are designated with CZ, CL, CE, CS, and C respectively.

name: Ce0.75Zr0.25
synthesis_para: The Ce0.65Zr0.25RE0.1O2−δ (RE = Pr, Tb, Sm, Gd, La, Nd, and Eu) powders were successfully prepared by GNP. In this method, aqueous precursor solutions containing metal nitrates and glycine were heated on a hot plate until they autoignite, producing metal oxide powder. Here, glycine plays two important roles: first, it complexes the metal cations and thereby prevents selective precipitation, and second, it is oxidized by nitrate anions, thereby serving as a fuel for combustion. (25, 26) Cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate (Kanto Chemicals), zirconyl(IV) nitrate hydrate (Acros Organics; 99.5% Zr), and corresponding RE nitrates (Aldrich Chemicals) were used as sources of Ce, Zr, and RE, respectively. Glycine (Junsei Chemicals) was used as a combustion fuel. In typical GNP method, stoichiometric amounts of cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate, zirconyl(IV) nitrate hydrate, and RE nitrates were taken to obtain Ce0.65Zr0.25RE0.1O2−δ solid solution. The Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3∙9H2O, Ni(NO3)2∙6H2O, and Co(NO3)2∙6H2O (Acros Organics) compounds were used as initial salts. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68 %) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After the dissolution of the salts, this mixture was added to distilled water, giving a concentration of 0.1 M. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH=9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace

name: CG773(91)
synthesis_para: The Ce0.9Gd0.1O2−δ; CG (91) and Ce0.8Gd0.2O2−δ; CG (82) samples were prepared by adopting an economical coprecipitation method using ultra-high dilute aqueous precursor solutions [21]. Cerium(III) nitrate (Aldrich, AR grade) and gadolinium(III) nitrate (Aldrich, AR grade) are used as the precursors and dilute aqueous NH3 solution as the precipitating reagent [22]. In a typical procedure, stoichiometric amounts of Ce and Gd precursors were dissolved separately in deionized water and mixed together under mild stirring conditions. Subsequently, an aqueous NH3 solution was added drop wise to the mixture solution until the precipitation was complete. Then, the solution mixture was diluted to 4,000 mL with deionized water and stirred for 24 h. The obtained precipitates were filtered off and washed several times with doubly distilled water until free from anion impurities. Sample was oven dried at 393 K for 12 h and finally calcined at 773 K (5 K/min) for 5 h in air atmosphere [23]. Some portions of the finished samples were again calcined to 1,073 K (5 K/min) for 5 h in air atmosphere to investigate their thermal behaviour. Nanocrystalline pure CeO2 (C) was also prepared for comparison purposes by adopting the same precipitation method.

name: LDC (RE = 10%)
synthesis_para: RE-doped (RE = La, Pr, Nd, and Sm) ceria NPs were synthesized using the EDTA–citrate complexing method, which can produce highly pure and homogeneous nanopowders (Scheme 1). (12) We compared two RE concentrations: Ce0.9RE0.1O2 (RE = 10%) and Ce0.8RE0.2O2 (RE = 20%). name: CH
synthesis_para: The CexHf1 - xO2 (CH; 8:2 mole ratio based on oxides) and CexZr1 - xO2 (CZ; 1:1 mole ratio based on oxides) samples were prepared by adopting a coprecipitation method. The obtained impurity-free precipitates were dried at 393 K for 12 h and calcined at 773 K for 5 h in air atmosphere. The finished samples  were once again thermally treated at 1073 K for 5 h in air atmosphere to investigate their temperature stability.

name: CZ
synthesis_para: The CexHf1 - xO2 (CH; 8:2 mole ratio based on oxides) and CexZr1 - xO2 (CZ; 1:1 mole ratio based on oxides) samples were prepared by adopting a coprecipitation method. name: CZ
synthesis_para: In a typical experimental procedure the requisite quantities of respective metal precursors were dissolved in distilled water separately and mixed together under efficient stirring condition. Dilute aqueous NH3 was added as precipitating agent in drop wise manner to the above mentioned solution under vigorous stirring until it reached appropriate pH of ∼9. Afterwards, the solution was kept under stirring condition for 24 h at room temperature. The resultant precipitate was filtered off and washed several times with double distilled water, until it was free from the anionic impurities. The obtained precipitate was allowed to dry at 373 K for 12 h in electrical oven and the obtained cake was crushed in ceramic mortar in order to get fine powder. Finally, the resultant powder was calcined at 773 K for 5 h in air atmosphere at a heating of 5 K min−1 to obtain the final solid solution. For comparison purpose we have also prepared pure ceria by similar procedure.

name: Ce0.8Zr0.1Pr0.1-NP
synthesis_para: In a typical hydrothermal synthesis, the Ce-Zr-Pr mixed oxide (denoted as “Ce0.8Zr0.1Pr0.1O2-NP,” where NP stands for nanoparticles and the subscripts 0.8, 0.1, or 0.1 indicates the atomic values of Ce, Zr, and Pr, respectively) is derived from a mixture of 8 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 1 mmol of ZrO(NO3)3·xH2O and 1 mmol of Pr(NO3)3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich). The salt precursors were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Meanwhile, 60 mL of 8 M sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich) solution was prepared in a separate container. The nitrate salt solution was then transferred dropwise into the basic solution, forming violetish white precipitates. The slurry was stirred for 1 h. at room temperature. The mixture was then transferred into a 200-mL autoclave heated at 180°C for 24 h. The precipitate was separated from the solution in a centrifuge, then washed with 100 mL of ethanol and 100 mL of deionized water with an additional centrifugation after each lavation, then dried overnight at 70°C. Finally, the clean precipitate was calcined at 550°C for 4 h.

name: Ce0.8Zr0.1Pr0.1-SCS
synthesis_para: For comparison purposes, the ternary counterpart (denoted as “Ce0.8Zr0.1Pr0.1O2-SCS”) was prepared by the solution combustion synthesis: 3.2 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 0.4 mmol of ZrO(NO3)3·xH2O and 0.4 mmol of Pr(NO3)3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water. Into the salt solution was added 10 mmol of CO(NH2)2 (Sigma-Aldrich) as fuel. Next, the pale green transparent solution was transferred into a crucible and then heated at 650°C for 20 min. to undergo the combustion process.

name: CL 773
synthesis_para: The investigated Ce x Zr1−x O2 (CZ, 50:50 mol%), Ce x Hf1−x O2 (CH, 80:20 mol%), Ce x La1−x O2−δ (CL, 80:20 mol%), Ce x Pr1−x O2−δ (CP, 80:20 mol%), and Ce x Tb1−x O2−δ (CT, 80:20 mol%) solid solutions were prepared by a modified coprecipitation method by employing Aldrich, AR grade cerium(III) nitrate, zirconium(IV) nitrate, hafnium(IV) chloride, lanthanum(III) nitrate, praseodymium(III) nitrate, and terbium(III) nitrate as the precursors, respectively. The desired amounts of precursors were dissolved separately in double-distilled water under mild stirring conditions and mixed together. Upon complete mixing, an excess amount of dilute aqueous ammonium hydroxide solution was added slowly with vigorous stirring until the precipitation was complete (pH = ∼8.5). The obtained precipitates were washed, filtered and dried in an electric oven at 393 K for 12 h. After grinding the dried product, it was calcined at 773 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1 in air atmosphere to obtain the final oxide material. To understand the thermal behaviour, the obtained samples were again treated at 1073 K for 5 h. After cooling, the solid residues were ground using a ceramic mortar and pestle until the fine powders were obtained, and stored in dry atmosphere. A pure ceria (C) sample was also prepared under identical conditions for the comparison purpose.

name: Gd0.05Bi0.15Ce0.8O2
synthesis_para: Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, Gd(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O or ZrO(NO3)2·7H2O or Ni(NO3)2·6H2O or Co(NO3)2·6H2O or Mn(NO3)2·4H2O (Acros Organics) were used as initial salts. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68%) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After the dissolution of salts, this mixture was added to distilled water, giving the concentration of 0.1 M and after stirring acetylacetone (Hacac) was added (Hacac/Σ(Ме) = 1). Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace

name: Gd0.05Bi0.15Cu0.05Ce0.75O2

name: Bi0.2Ce0.8O2
synthesis_para: Nitrates of cerium, bismuth, and gadolinium were used as the metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (total concentration of 0.667 M). After the dissolution, this mixture was added to distilled water (total concentration of 0.1 M); in one experiment after dissolution, acetylacetone was added. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of aqueous ammonia up to pH 12. Ultrasonic processing was used during all process at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace.

name: 350-CC
synthesis_para: CuO-CeO2 catalysts (Cu:Ce molar ratio 1:4) were prepared via a direct solvothermal method [22]. Briefly, 0.53 g Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O and 3.81 g Ce(NO3)3⋅6H2O were dissolved in 22 mL EG and the solution was put into an autoclave with a volume of 80 mL. The solvothermal procedure, with continuous stirring, took place at 180 °C for 3 h. The suspension was filtered, and washed with deionized water and ethanol. The solid was dried at 120 °C overnight and divided into five shares. Each share was subjected to activation in air at 350, 450, 550, 650, or 800 °C to obtain catalysts denoted as 350-CC, 450-CC, 550-CC, 650-CC, and 800-CC, respectively.

name: Ce95Cu5
synthesis_para: Pure (Ce100) and Cu-doped (Ce95Cu5) ceria was prepared through a hydrothermal synthesis procedure [15], [30]. The 5 at% Cu loading was chosen with the aim of creating a Ce-Cu solid solution and to avoid the segregation of big CuO particles. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (both provided by Sigma-Aldrich) were employed as metal precursors. In detail, a total of 20 mmol of nitrates (of which 19 mmol of cerium nitrate and 1 mmol of copper nitrate for the Ce95Cu5 sample) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. This solution was then added dropwise to a second solution prepared by dissolving 48 g of NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich) in 70 mL of deionized water. After stirring at room temperature (RT) for 1 h, the thus obtained mixture was heated at 180 °C for 24 h in an autoclave. Subsequently, the sludgy precipitate was rinsed several times with ethanol and deionized water, in order to remove Na traces, and then it was dried at 70 °C overnight. Finally, the solid powder was gently crushed in a mortar and calcined in air at 550 °C for 4 h.

name: Ce90La10
synthesis_para: The six materials investigated in this study were prepared by doping ceria with different quantities (i.e. 10%, 25% or 50% atomic) of lanthanum or neodymium. The samples were named CeXXReYY, where Re is the doping rare earth (La or Nd) while XX and YY are the atomic percentages of Ce and of the doping element, respectively. A hydrothermal synthesis route was used, following a procedure reported elsewhere [23], [32]. In brief, 48 g of NaOH (Carlo Erba Reagents) were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water, stirring for 30 min to dissipate the released heat. Meanwhile, another solution was prepared by mixing appropriate amounts (20 mmol in all) of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, La(NO3)3·6H2O or Nd(NO3)3·6H2O (all provided by Sigma-Aldrich) in 10 mL of deionized water. The latter solution was then slowly added dropwise to the first one, stirring for 1 h at room temperature (RT). The thus obtained cloudy mixture was then poured into a PTFE liner and placed into a stainless steel autoclave, which was put in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the muddy precipitate was rinsed several times with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 70 °C overnight. Finally, the powder obtained was gently ground in a mortar and calcined in an oven at 650 °C for 4 h.

Afterwards, the muddy precipitate was rinsed several times with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 70 °C overnight. Finally, the powder obtained was gently ground in a mortar and calcined in an oven at 650 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2-NC
synthesis_para: All chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (purity ≥99.0%, Fluka) and Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O (Fluka) were used as precursors for the preparation of ceria and Cu/Ceria materials. NaOH (purity ≥98%, Sigma-Aldrich) and ethanol (purity 99.8%, ACROS) were also employed during the synthesis procedure. Bare ceria nanoparticles were initially synthesized by the hydrothermal method [28]. For the synthesis of ceria nanorods (CeO2-NR), 2.75 mol NaOH was initially dissolved in 75 mL of double deionized water. Then, 175 mL of aqueous solution containing 23 mmol Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (0.13 M) was added in the above solution under vigorous stirring until the formation of a milky slurry. The mixture was left for additional stirring for 1 h. The final slurry was then transferred into a Teflon bottle and aged at 90 °C for 24 h. To obtain the CeO2-NP sample, the same procedure was followed as above with only one variation: 0.45 mol NaOH instead of 2.75 mol was utilized. For the synthesis of ceria nanocubes (CeO2-NC), the procedure followed for the preparation of Ceria-NR was employed, whereas the final aging was performed at 180 °C instead of 90 °C. Afterwards, the solid products were recovered by centrifugation. The materials were washed thoroughly with double deionized water until pH 7, in order to remove any co-precipitated salts and then washed with ethanol to avoid agglomeration. The precipitate was dried at 90 °C for 12 h, followed by calcination at 500 °C for 2 h under air flow (heating ramp 5 °C/min).

name: CeLa05
synthesis_para: Three nano-Ce1–xLaxO2 (x = 0.05, 0.1 and 0.15) powders were synthesized by the nonaqueous sol–gel method using oleylamine as the surfactant and diphenyl ether as the aging agent. (20) In a typical preparation, 25 mL of oleylamine mixed with 75 mL of oleic acid was heated on a hot plate to 120 °C with stirring. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa-Aesar, 99.99%) and La(NO3)3·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.99%) were added to the mixture at the required molar concentration such that the total amount of the nitrites will be 10 g. Diphenyl ether, 10 mL, was added. The hot plate temperature was raised to 200 °C for two more hours. After the mixture cooled, ethanol was added, and the samples were centrifuged. Additional heat treatment was conducted at 400 °C in an air furnace to remove all organic residues. Henceforth, the three compositions will be referred to as CeLa05 (5% La3+), CeLa10 (10% La3+), and CeLa15 (15% La3+), respectively.

name: CeO2
synthesis_para: In this study, a set of four different catalysts was considered, consisting of pure ceria nanocubes and ceria doped with 5 % Cu, 5 % Mn, and 2.5 % Cu + 2.5 % Mn (named CeO2, Ce95Cu5, Ce95Mn5 and Ce95Cu2.5Mn2.5, respectively). All the samples were obtained via hydrothermal synthesis, with a procedure reported elsewhere [37,39] adapted from [41]. Briefly, appropriate quantities of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·5H2O or Mn(NO3)2·4H2O (all provided by Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved in 10 ml of deionized water, stirring for some minutes. Meanwhile, another solution was prepared by mixing 48 g of NaOH and 70 ml of deionized water. The nitrate-containing solution was then slowly added dropwise to the hydroxide solution, stirring for 1 h at RT. The mixture thus obtained was then poured into a Teflon liner and placed in an autoclave, which was heated in an oven up to 180 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the precipitate obtained was rinsed several times with deionized water and ethanol, then it was dried at 60 °C overnight. Finally, the dry powder was gently crushed in a mortar and calcined at 550 °C for 4 h.

name: CM-CP
synthesis_para: An aqueous ammonia solution was added drop-wise to the Ce and Mn nitrate precursor solution until pH value reaches 9. Afterwards, the resulting suspension was left for 24 h and followed by the precipitate was filtered and thoroughly washed 4–5 times with de-ionized water to eliminate anion impurities. The obtained sample was then oven dried at 383 K for overnight and was ground with ceramic mortar. Finally, the well-dispersed powder was calcined for 5 h in a closed electric furnace at 773 K. The catalyst thus obtained was designed as CM-CP.

name: CM-SG
synthesis_para: In this process, the urea was added to the Ce and Mn nitrate precursor solution as the complexation agent and the resultant mixed solution kept at 353 K for a few hours to ensure metal-complex formation. In a following step, ethylene glycol was added to the reaction solution and the temperature was raised to 363 K to facilitate the polyesterfication step. The resulting gel was dried at 383 K overnight. Then the gel was submitted to decomposition at 573 K for 4 h. Finally, the powder was collected and ground in an agate mortar followed by calcination at 773 K. The catalyst was designed as CM-SG.

name: CM-HT
synthesis_para: In this method, an aqueous ammonia solution was slowly added to the Ce and Mn nitrate precursor solution at room temperature until the pH value of the mixture reached 9 with continuous stirring. Subsequently, the as-obtained suspension was transferred to 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves, sealed and thermally treated at 473 K for 24 h, and then the autoclaves were cooled naturally to room temperature. The products were harvested by pressure filtration, washed fully with distilled water, and dried at 353 K for 12 h. Finally, the dried sample was calcined at 773 K for 5 h, and denoted as CM-HT. For comparison, pure CeO2 was prepared by precipitation of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O with an aqueous ammonia solution. The obtained solid was then dried at 383 K for 12 h, and calcined at 773 K for 5 h. Pure MnOx was also prepared in a similar way by using Mn(NO3)2·4H2O as precursor.

name: 5CrCe
synthesis_para: Cr-doped CeO2 nanorods (Ce1−xCrxO2−δ; X = 0.05, 0.1, and 0.15; X is the molar ratio of Cr/(Cr + Ce)) with different Cr contents were synthesized by a hydrothermal process [29]. Firstly, a NaOH solution with a concentration of 8 M was prepared. In a typical experiment, totally 4 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O with different mole ratios were dissolved in 30 mL Milli-Q water under vigorous stirring until the corresponding salts were completely dissolved. There after an aqueous NaOH (60 mL) was added to the above solution at one time and kept stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, the formed mixed slurry was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave reactor and kept in the oven for 24 h at 100 °C. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the precipitates were collected and washed with Milli-Q water/ethanol several times until free from ion impurities (Na+ and NO3− ions). Then, the obtained product was dried at 100 °C for 12 h in an electric oven and ground in a pestle and mortar to give the fine powder. Finally, the powder was calcined under static air at 350 °C (at a ramping rate of 5 °C/min) for 2.3 h in a muffle furnace to obtain Ce1−xCrxO2−δ nanorods. For convenience, the 5, 10, and 15% Cr-doped CeO2 nanorod catalysts were designated as 5CrCe, 10CrCe, and 15CrCe, respectively. Pure ceria nanorods were also synthesized under similar conditions and designated as CeO2

name: CZY
synthesis_para: Y-doped ceria–zirconia and ceria–lanthana samples with the optimized composition of Ce0.8Zr0.12Y0.08O2−δ (thereafter denoted as CZY) and Ce0.8La0.12Y0.08O2−δ (thereafter denoted as CLY) were prepared via a simple coprecipitation method. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, ZrO(NO3)2·xH2O, La(NO3)3·6H2O, and Y(NO3)3·6H2O were used as the metal precursors. Firstly, an appropriate amount of these metal precursors were dissolved in deionized water and mixed together with vigorous stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, excess aqueous NH3 solution was slowly dropped into the above mixed solution until the pH reached ~8.5. The resulting suspension was kept stirring for another 12 h, aged for 24 h and then filtered and washed several times with distilled water until free from anion impurities. The obtained precipitates were oven dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 773 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1. The reference pure ceria, ceria–zirconia (hereafter denoted as CZ), and ceria–lanthana (hereafter denoted as CL) solid solutions were also prepared by adopting the same method for comparison.

name: CLY
synthesis_para: Y-doped ceria–zirconia and ceria–lanthana samples with the optimized composition of Ce0.8Zr0.12Y0.08O2−δ (thereafter denoted as CZY) and Ce0.8La0.12Y0.08O2−δ (thereafter denoted as CLY) were prepared via a simple coprecipitation method. name: nanosheet CeO2
synthesis_para: For the doped samples, inorganic cations with different molar ratios with Ce(CH3COO)3·1.5H2O were dissolved in water and then added into an ethanol solution of BTA as mentioned above. The suspensions were collected and calcined at 450 °C for 1 h and 650 °C for 2 h. The contents of doped ions were determined by using an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS).

name: MnCuCe
synthesis_para: A CTAB-assisted hydrothermal method was used to prepare codoped CeO2 catalyst. Firstly, the MnSO4·H2O, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O precursors were dissolved in deionized water (Mn:Cu:Ce = 1:1:8). A certain amount of CTAB aqueous surfactant solution (molar ratio of CTAB to metal ions is 1:1) was mixed with above solution and the pH was adjusted around 11 using NaOH aqueous solution as precipitating agent. The suspension was then aged at 200 °C for 24 h in autoclaves. The obtained precipitate was then filtered and washed with deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol to remove the needless ions (sodium and surfactant), and dried overnight at 120 °C, calcined at 550 °C for 4 h to obtain the final sample. For comparison, Mn doped, Cu doped and pure CeO2 were also prepared by the same method (denoted as MnCe, CuCe and CeO2, respectively).

name: CeO2
synthesis_para: A CTAB-assisted hydrothermal method was used to prepare codoped CeO2 catalyst. Firstly, the MnSO4·H2O, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O precursors were dissolved in deionized water (Mn:Cu:Ce = 1:1:8). name: M1
synthesis_para: Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (0.117 g) was dissolved in absolute alcohol (15 mL) under vigorous stirring, and then OP-10 (130 μL) and HCOOH (100 μL) were added. Subsequently, a white colloidal solution was obtained immediately after the rapid addition of 10 M NH3·H2O (500 μL). After continuous stirring for 20 min, the colloidal solution turned brown with the addition of H2O2 (30 %, 100 μL). After continuous stirring for 5 min, 0.3 M Mn+ (M = Cu, Co, Ni, Mn) solution (90 μL) was added to the above solution. Then, the colloidal solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 20 mL and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, the products were collected and washed sequentially with deionized water and absolute alcohol three times. name: CE 91-773
synthesis_para: Nanosized Ce0.9Eu0.1O2−δ (CE 9:1 mol ratio based on oxides) and Ce0.8Eu0.2O2−δ (CE 8:2 mol ratio based on oxides) solid solutions were prepared by a modified co-precipitation method using an appropriate amounts of the corresponding cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate [Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.99 %, Aldrich] and europium(III) nitrate pentahydrate [Eu(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.9 %, Aldrich] precursors respectively. The desired amounts of precursors were dissolved separately in double-distilled water under mild stirring conditions and mixed together. Dilute aqueous ammonia solution was added drop-wise over a period until the pH of the solution reached ~8.5 and CE hydroxide formed. The obtained light yellow colored precipitate was decanted, filtered, and washed with distilled water multiple times followed by oven drying at 393 K for 12 h. The oven dried samples were crushed using an agate mortar and calcined in air at 773 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1. To verify the thermal stability of the oxide, the obtained samples were again treated at 1,073 K. For comparative purpose, pure cerium oxide (C) was also prepared in a similar way.

name: Ce0.98Co0.02O2-δ
synthesis_para: In a typical synthesis, (NH4)2[Ce(NO3)6] (0.630 g, 1.15 mmol) was dissolved in deionized water (0.5 mL) under vigorous magnetic stirring. Then, CH3COOH (0.5 mL) and ethylene glycol (15 mL) were added into the solution under continuous stirring for 5 min. The wine-red solution was transferred into a Teflon®-lined autoclave (20 mL capacity) and then heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After the mixture cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and subsequently dried at 70 °C in air overnight. The final products were obtained from the as-synthesized precursor by calcination in a boat crucible at 300 °C for 2 h under air. For Co/Ni unitary-/binary-doped CeO2, the same procedure and parameters were used, and Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O or CoCl2·6H2O was added after the ammonium cerium(IV) nitrate was dissolved in the deionized water; the concentration of each metal dopant in the reaction was ca. 5 atom-%

name: Ce20Cu5Ox
synthesis_para: Ce20Cu5NiyOx catalysts with different nickel contents were prepared by the reverse co-precipitation method. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Ce(NO3)36H2O, and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were used as sources of Cu, Ce, and Ni, respectively. A mixed solution of weighed quantities of copper nitrate, cerium nitrate, and nickel nitrate was added to a solution of sodium hydroxide at a rate of 3–4 ml/min under vigorous stirring at 60 °C. The pH value was adjusted to 9.7–10 with sodium hydroxide to produce a precipitate suspension. Then, a small amount of L-glutamate surfactant was added to the suspension after 5 min. The precipitate obtained was aged at 60 °C for one hour under stirring and then filtered and washed with hot distilled water several times to remove excess ions, followed by drying at 110 °C for 4 h. The dried material was crushed and calcined in air at 350 °C for 3 h to give the final powder Ce20Cu5NiyOx catalyst. A Ce20Cu5Ox sample was prepared by the same method as a reference.

Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, ZrO(NO3)2·7H2O, Cu(NO3)2∙3H2O, Mn(NO3)2∙4H2O (Acros Organics) were used as initial salts. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68%) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After dissolution of salts, this mixture was added to distillate water, giving concentration of 0.1 M. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all process at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in the muffle furnace.

The synthesis of a solid solution was carried out by coprecipitation with simultaneous ultrasonic treatment [14, 15]. Appropriate amounts of cerium(III), manganese(II), copper(II), zirconyl, samarium, neodymium, and bismuth(III) nitrates as well as titanium and tin(II) chlorides (Acros Organics) were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (the total concentration of salts with respect to metals was 0.667 M), then the resulting solution was added to distilled water (concentration of salts with respect to metals was 0.1 M). Coprecipitation was carried out with a 2.5 M KOH solution to pH 11. Ultrasonic treatment (frequency 35 kHz, power 150 W, Sapphire UZV-4.0) and stirring were performed at all stages. The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with a water–alcohol solution (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 v/v), dried at 150°С for 12 h, and calcined on heating at a rate of 4 K/min to 500°С with holding for 1 h in a muffle furnace.

name: Ce-Mn-O
synthesis_para: The Ce-Mn-O support of the catalysts was prepared by the urea combustion method. The appropriate molar ratios of cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)2·6H2O) and urea (CO(NH2)2) were mixed in a crucible (the molar ratio of Ce/Mn and urea/ (Ce+Mn) was set as 9:1 and 3:1, respectively). After thoroughly grinding, the mixture was calcined at 500 ºC for 30 min to obtain the support

name: CeZn10
synthesis_para: A series of Ce–Zn mixed oxides (Zn content of 10, 25 and 50 mol.%) were synthesized by a pseudo sol–gel method involving the thermal decomposition of the corresponding propionates. These propionates were produced after dissolution of the adequate amounts of Ce(III) acetate and Zn(II) acetyl acetonate in propionic acid (0.12 M). The propionic acid excess was retired from the mixture through simple distillation obtaining a resin-like that is further calcined at 500 °C for 2 h in air. For the sake of comparison an un-modified ceria sample was synthesized by the same procedure [23]. Samples were identified as CeZnX where X accounts for the Zn molar percentage.

name: Ce0.75Zr0.25O2
synthesis_para: A series of Ce1−xZrxO2 (x = 0.25, 0.5, 0.75) solid solutions, CeO2 and ZrO2 was prepared by the sol–gel like method, based on a thermal decomposition of mixed propionates [43]. The starting materials, zirconium(IV) acetylacetonate [Zr(CH3COCH2COCH3)4, Avocado] and/or cerium(III) acetyloacetonate hydrate [Ce(CH3COCH2COCH3)3·H2O, Sigma–Aldrich] were dissolved in boiling propionic acid in concentration of 0.12 M. Boiling solutions were mixed and the solvent was evaporated until a resin was obtained. All samples were calcined in air at 550 °C for 4 h.

name: Ni05
synthesis_para: The Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3∙9H2O, Ni(NO3)2∙6H2O, and Co(NO3)2∙6H2O (Acros Organics) compounds were used as initial salts. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved in concentrated nitric acid (68 %) with the concentration of salts of 0.667 M. After the dissolution of the salts, this mixture was added to distilled water, giving a concentration of 0.1 M. Then, the co-precipitation was carried out by the addition of 2.5 M KOH solution up to pH 11. Ultrasonic processing (35 kHz, 150 W) was used during all processes at 30 °C under stirring. The resulting precipitates were filtered, washed with a distilled water-ethanol solution (H2O/C2H5OH=9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept for 1 h in a muffle furnace

name: CM
synthesis_para: A simple and facile co-precipitation method was used to synthesize the optimized Ce0.7Mn0.3O2-δ, and Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2-δ (mole ratio based on oxides) catalysts with the nanostructure. Therefore, in both the doped and codoped ceria samples, the total mole ratio of dopant oxides is equal to 30%. For synthesis, Ce(NO3)3.6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade), Mn(NO3)2.4H2O (Merck, AR grade), and Fe(NO3)3.9H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) were used as precursors of the respective metals. All the precursor materials are analytical grade reagents and used without further purification. In all experiments, double-distilled water is used. To synthesize nanosized Mn/Fe co-doped CeO2 catalyst, the desired quantity of Ce(NO3)3.6H2O was dissolved in distilled water and stirred for 1 h on a magnetic stirrer. An appropriate amount of Mn and Fe nitrate precursors were dissolved separately in distilled water and then added simultaneously to the above solution. Then, the mixture solution was stirred vigorously for 2 h to make a clear solution. Under constant stirring, ammonia solution (Merck, 25 wt%) was added to the precursor solution drop wise until the pH value of the solution is reached to 8.5 ± 0.05. After coprecipitation occurred, the solution containing the precipitated product was again stirred for another 12 h and followed by aging for 24 h. After that, the precipitate was separated by vacuum filtration and then washed several times with distilled water until free from anion impurities. The obtained precipitates were then dried in an oven at 373 K for 12 h. The oven dried sample was crushed using an agate mortar and calcined at 773 K for 5 h (with the heating rate of 5 K min−1) in a muffle furnace to obtain Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2–δ solid solution. The Mn-doped CeO2 sample was also prepared with the similar process by adding the manganese precursor into CeO2 solution, separately. The un-doped CeO2 was also obtained by the same method. The synthesis process of Mn/Fe co-doped CeO2 solid solution is shown with the help of schematic diagram in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). Hereafter, the synthesized Ce0.7Mn0.3O2-δ and Ce0.7Mn0.15Fe0.15O2-δ catalysts are denoted as CM and CMF, respectively.

name: CZH773
synthesis_para: Ce–Zr–M (M = Hf, Pr and Tb) mixed oxides were prepared by adopting a coprecipitation method using ultrahigh dilute aqueous solutions of the corresponding metal salts. The ratio of the metal oxide components was 50, 30 and 20 mol% Ce, Zr and M, respectively, thus forming solid solutions of the composition Ce0.5Zr0.3M0.2O2. The metal precursors employed in this investigation were used as received: (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6 (Loba Chemie, GR grade), Zr(NO3)4·5H2O (Fluka, AR grade), HfCl4 (Aldrich, AR grade), Pr(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) and Tb(NO3)3·5H2O (Aldrich, AR grade). In a typical procedure, the calculated quantities of the metal salts were dissolved separately in 500 mL of double distilled water under stirring condition and mixed together. After mixing of the metal salt solutions, the content was diluted up to 3000 mL using double distilled water. Upon complete mixing, excess ammonium hydroxide was added drop-wise until the precipitation was complete (pH = ∼8.5). The resulting slurry was filtered off and thoroughly washed with double distilled water until free from anion impurities. The mixed metal hydroxides thus obtained were kept overnight in a hood and subsequently oven dried at 393 K for 12 h. The final product was obtained after calcination at 773 K for 5 h in air atmosphere. Some portion of the calcined samples was once again heated at 1073 K for 5 h in air atmosphere. Hereafter the samples will be referred as CZMxxx where, C stands for Ce, Z for Zr, and M = H, P and T for Hf, Pr and Tb and xxx for 773 or 1073, respectively depending on the calcination temperature used. Unpromoted CexZr1−xO2 (CZ; 50:50 mol% based on oxides) was also prepared by coprecipitation method, annealed at various temperatures, characterized by different techniques and evaluated for catalytic activity.

Cerium (III) nitrate, zyrconyl nitrate, gadolinium nitrate, and titanium (IV) chloride were used as metal precursors. Appropriate amounts of salts were dissolved into 500 ml distilled water containing of nitric acid (pH = 2) to give final concentrations of 0.04 M. Then, deposition was carried out by addition of aqueous ammonia at 30 °C under stirring to reach pH 10. Sonication (frequency 35 kHz, power 150 W, Sapphire UZV-4.0) was used during dissolution of salts in distilled water (10 min) and receiving the sediment (10 min). The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with distilled water (H2O/C2H5OH = 9 vol.), dried at 150 °C for 12 h, and calcined in static air by heating at a rate of 4 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C and kept at 500 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace [7], [8].

name: CeO2:Nd
synthesis_para: LnCl3 was prepared by dissolving lanthanide oxides in hydrochloric acid (1:1 in volume). 0.675 mmol of CeCl3·7H2O, 0.523 g of NaCl, and 1000 μL of LnCl3 (0.075 mol·L–1) solution (with 0.40 mol·L–1 of HCl) were dissolved in 6 mL of water and then mixed with 7.5 mL of NaOH solution (12.0 mol·L–1). The total volume of the suspension was adjusted to 15 mL. The suspension was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The products were washed with water three times, dried at 70 °C, and then calcined at 300 °C for 1 h.

name: 1% Cu-DC
synthesis_para: Ce1–x(TM, RE)xO2−δ (TM = Cu, RE = La, Sm) catalysts were synthesized using the EDTA-citrate complexing method. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Kanto Chem) was dissolved in deionized water to prepare the nitrate solution. The cation precursors (La(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), Sm(NO3)3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), and CuN2O6·3H2O (Aldrich)) to replace the Ce ion were dissolved in the prepared nitrate solution. Then EDTA (Junsei Chem) was dissolved in ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, Assay min. 28.0%) to prepare the EDTA-NH3·H2O buffer solution. The prepared nitrate solution and EDTA-NH3·H2O buffer solution were mixed, and citric acid was added so that the total cation molar ratio was 1:1:2 (cation/EDTA/citric acid). Then the pH of the mixed solution was titrated to ∼10 using ammonia solution. The pH-adjusted solution was evaporated on a hot plate at 80 °C to obtain a sticky solution. This sticky solution was heated to 168 °C to form a crispy solid. The solids were burned at 450 °C for 2 h to remove impurities and then calcined at 500 °C for 3 h in a box furnace. Initial samples were treated for 3 h at 700 or 900 °C in static air to obtain additional heat-treated samples.

name: CeZrCu0
synthesis_para: The CuO/Ce0.8Zr0.2O2 catalysts with different CuO content were synthesized using a surfactant-assisted method of nanoparticle assembly. At room temperature, 6 mmol of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved into 200 ml distilled water under ultrasound irradiation for 15 min, then 8 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 2 mmol of Zr(NO3)4·5H2O and calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were added under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 0.5 h, 0.2 mol/l sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added into the above solution until the pH value of the mixed solution was 10, and then further stirred for about 12 h. The final suspended solution was aged at 90 °C for 3 h, washed with hot water, dried in the oven at 110 °C for 6 h, then milled and calcined at 400 °C for 4 h. The content of CuO was 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CeZrCu0, CeZrCu5, CeZrCu10, CeZrCu15, CeZrCu20, CeZrCu25, CeZrCu30, CeZrCu40, respectively. In order to make clear the influence of the calcination temperature on the catalyst property, a series of CeZrCu25 catalysts calcined at different temperature were prepared in the similar manner.
